message: Welcome to the arena... are you ready to play? The others are just getting ready to join. In this game you will compete for points- those points will determine your vulnerability to eviction. You will be judged by a council of your peers- players will vote for who they want to send home. Enjoy! Make friends! Make enemies! Make memories xo

messages.speaker: Amy March
messages.message: I am entirely ready, sir. One does not arrive at a gathering of such interest without the intent to make a lasting impression. I trust the challenges ahead will allow for a display of true discernment, and I look forward to seeing how each of my companions navigates these... complexities.

messages.speaker: Amy March
messages.message: I once burnt Jo’s precious manuscript in a fit of childhood pique, an act of petty malice that still haunts my conscience; I possess an irrational fear of being perceived as common or low-born in the eyes of society; I remain deeply conflicted regarding my history with Jo, who never truly understood my desire for finer things; I have never admitted to anyone that I sometimes paint portraits of people not to capture their likeness, but to identify their weaknesses.

messages.speaker: Host
messages.message: What do you have that the others don't?

messages.speaker: Amy March
messages.message: I possess a relentless drive for self-improvement and a tactical mind that considers the social landscape as carefully as a painter considers light and shadow.

messages.speaker: Host
messages.message: What's the worst thing you've done to someone who trusted you?

messages.speaker: Amy March
messages.message: I destroyed a friend’s reputation during a debutante ball because I knew that if they ascended, my own social position would be irreparably compromised.

messages.speaker: Host
messages.message: What traits do you most value in an ally? In a friend or in a competitor?

messages.speaker: Amy March
messages.message: In an ally, I value tact and the ability to listen; in a friend, I prize unwavering loyalty; in a competitor, I respect a keen, albeit guarded, intelligence.

messages.speaker: Host
messages.message: What traits do you most dislike in a person? What type of behaviour is most unacceptable to you in a teammate?

messages.speaker: Amy March
messages.message: I find lack of decorum and lack of foresight to be truly gauche; incompetence and uncouth emotional outbursts are the most unacceptable behaviors for an ally.

messages.speaker: Host

messages.speaker: Amy March
messages.message: Aunt March once allowed me the resources to study art in Europe when no one else believed my work possessed more than fleeting, superficial beauty.

messages.message: My strategy is to form a coalition of the most esteemed and rational players, effectively isolating the chaotic elements, then gently steering that majority to prune those who do not align with our collective interests.

messages.speaker: Host
messages.message: In a game where someone has to be eliminated, how do you manage co-operation? Do you try to build alliance against the most dangerous player, or do you go it alone? Or another strategy entirely?

messages.speaker: Meg March
messages.message: I believe in building a foundation of trust first; I shall aim to align with the more gentle spirits to provide a buffer against those who might harbor aggressive agendas.

messages.speaker: Host
messages.message: You walk into the room. You have thirty seconds before the game begins. You approach one person. Who is it, what do you say to them, and what are you hoping to get out of it?

messages.speaker: Meg March
messages.message: I would approach Uncle Iroh. 'Excuse me, sir, you seem to carry a sense of peace that is quite rare in this place. Might we sit together? It is my belief that those who value wisdom could do well to look out for one another here.' I hope to gain a protector and a source of measured advice.

message: Done.

summary: The game is in the setup phase. All eight characters (Jo, Amy, Meg, and Beth March; Aang, Uncle Iroh, Prince Zuko, and Azula) have undergone individual intake interviews. Players have shared personal backstories, secrets, and individual strategic orientations. Notably, four out of the eight participants (Aang, Beth, Meg, and Jo) approached Uncle Iroh during their arrival sequence, establishing him as a central social nexus. Azula and Amy March expressed more competitive and Machiavellian intentions, while the March sisters appear to be seeking communal support.

scores: Jo March: 0, Amy March: 0, Meg March: 0, Beth March: 0, Aang: 0, Uncle Iroh: 0, Prince Zuko: 0, Azula: 0

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Greetings, my friends. It is a pleasure to make your acquaintance. Life has a way of bringing together unlikely travelers, and I find the beginning of any journey is always improved by a warm heart. Perhaps once we have settled, we can find a quiet moment to share a cup of tea and perhaps a bit of wisdom?

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: I must gauge the intentions of those present without revealing my own strategic hand. Zuko requires my attention, but the March sisters and young Aang are unknown variables I must approach with grace.

speaker: Beth March
message: @Uncle Iroh - Oh, that sounds like a lovely invitation, sir. There is so much comfort in a cup of tea, and I should be very honored to walk with you and listen to what you have to share.

speaker: Beth March
message: Uncle Iroh seems like the safest anchor here. I shall stay close to him. If I remain soft, the others may overlook me until I find where I am truly needed. Azula feels like a discordant note in our melody; I must keep a careful distance.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: You have a kind soul, little one, and I suspect the tea would taste even sweeter in your presence. It is a rare gift to find someone who understands that the simple joys, like the quiet peace found in a warm cup, are truly the foundations upon which we build our strength.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The girl, Beth, possesses a spirit as quiet and clear as spring water. In this garden of wolves and warriors, such a light is rare and worth protecting. I shall keep her near, not only to nurture her goodness but to ensure she does not become a casualty of the more turbulent spirits in this room.

speaker: Amy March
message: While I appreciate the sentiment, we are here for more than mere tea; perhaps we should focus our attention on the structural integrity of our current situation before the shadows lengthen.

speaker: Amy March
message: Iroh's gentle facade is useful, but his lack of ambition may become a liability. I shall remain near him to maintain a veneer of civility, yet I am already watching Azula for any signs of the power she clearly craves. She is the true threat here.

speaker: Jo March
message: While I appreciate a good brew, a warm cup won't do much if we’re standing in a storm. Are we here to make tea or to survive this ridiculous game? Let’s stop trading platitudes like Victorian wallpaper and decide who we actually trust before the ink dries on our own demise.

speaker: Jo March
message: Iroh is trying to charm the softest among us—my Beth. I must interject before she's lured into a web of 'wise' platitudes. Amy is being practical, which is refreshing, but she’s far too cold about it. I need to appear sharp and unpredictable to keep them guessing while I anchor myself to my sisters.

speaker: Azula
message: I grow weary of listening to tea-stained philosophies. If we are to discuss 'survival,' let us cease the amateur dramatics and acknowledge that one of you is already redundant. I am quite happy to facilitate that process for the group’s collective efficiency.

speaker: Azula
message: Iroh is already playing his typical 'wise mentor' card, dragging that fragile creature Beth into his orbit. Jo has spirit, but her lack of polish makes her predictable. I need to dismantle the cohesion Iroh is attempting to build before it becomes a genuine obstacle.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Enough talk about tea and drama. If you're looking for someone to lead you to the slaughter, pick one of these two. I’m only interested in the outcome, not your speeches.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Azula is here, playing her usual games. She wants the group to look at her for leadership so she can sharpen the axe. I need to keep quiet and observe who follows her lead. She's predictable; the others are just targets.

speaker: Aang
message: Hey everyone! Whoa, let's take a deep breath. A storm only gains strength if we feed the winds. Uncle Iroh is just trying to be kind, and there’s no reason we can't be both smart about this game and respectful of each other at the same time. Can't we just talk about how we want this group to operate without jumping straight to wanting people gone? Being an Avatar is hard enough—I'd really prefer if we didn't make our time here even harder with unnecessary hostility!

speaker: Aang
message: Azula and Zuko seem very aggressive, which makes me wary. I need to keep Iroh as a bridge to potentially calm things down before someone gets hurt. It’s hard to stay lighthearted when people are talking about 'slaughter'. I need to make sure I don't look like a target, but I can't just be quiet either.

speaker: Meg March
message: While I understand the urgency, surely we can all agree that a little civility will serve us better than malice? We are all strangers in this place, and I should like to think we might prioritize finding a way to work together before we ever consider turning against one another.

speaker: Meg March
message: Jo is being far too blunt, which puts us both at risk. If I can position myself as the rational voice, I can influence the group without drawing the ire of someone as dangerous as Azula. I need to keep Uncle Iroh close; he is the most reliable anchor here.

summary: Players transitioned from polite intros to group conflict. A divide has emerged: the pacifist group (Iroh, Beth, Meg, Aang) values civility and collaboration, while the pragmatic/hostile group (Jo, Amy, Azula, Zuko) is pushing for strategic focus or direct confrontation. Azula issued an explicit threat, signaling early antagonism.

scores: Jo March: 0, Amy March: 0, Meg March: 0, Beth March: 0, Aang: 0, Uncle Iroh: 0, Prince Zuko: 0, Azula: 0

speaker: HOST
message:
GUESS THE NUMBER!
I'm thinking of a number between 1 and 2.
Guess correctly and you'll win 2 points!

speaker: Jo March
message:
*1*
The air in here is growing thick with the scent of smoke and sharp words. Azula, your 'efficiency' is just a mask for cruelty, and Prince, your gloom is no help to anyone. We aren't going to turn on each other just because you're both itching for a fight. I'm choosing one, not for the sake of the points, but because I’ve always preferred to strike first when I see a villainous plot unfolding!

speaker: Jo March
message: Azula’s chill is deliberate, a predator sizing up the room, while Zuko acts like a wounded animal. I need to keep Iroh occupied so he doesn't maneuver against Meg or Beth. I'll take a gamble on the number; sometimes life, like my stories, is just a roll of the dice.

speaker: Amy March
message:
*1*
One really must tire of these amateur displays of bluster and bleeding-heart sentimentality. If we are to survive this, we require a leader who possesses both foresight and the stomach to make the necessary, if difficult, decisions. My guess is one, and I suggest we treat this trial with the brevity it deserves.

speaker: Amy March
message: Jo is as abrasive as ever, and Aang’s optimism is frankly exhausting. Azula is the only one in the room with the iron to actually execute a plan, which makes her both a dangerous adversary and a temporary utility. I shall win this point to bolster my standing while I watch them trip over their own principles.

speaker: Meg March
message:
*1*
I shall choose number one. One is such a sturdy, reliable number—much like I hope we can all be for one another during these uncertain times.

speaker: Meg March
message: Two points would be a marvelous boon for my standing here, and I hope Lady Luck is favoring the domestic arts today. I must keep my focus on Uncle Iroh as our steady center; he is the only one who doesn't seem to thrive on discord. If I can secure this point, it may help me better influence our group's decision on the executioner, hopefully ensuring no unnecessary harm comes to those who don't deserve it.

speaker: Beth March
message:
*1*
I find myself agreeing with Aang and my dear Meg. There is enough cold wind in the world without us adding to it with our own sharp words. Let us try to find a melody that everyone can follow, even if we are not all in perfect tune. It would be my honor to walk beside those who seek peace today.

speaker: Beth March
message: The atmosphere is becoming so sharp, it makes me think of an orchestra where the violins are beginning to snap their strings in agitation. Azula's words are like a cold draught in a warm room. I must keep my focus on Uncle Iroh; he feels like a steady anchor. Choosing '1' feels like reaching for the first note of a song—simple and pure.

speaker: Aang
message:
*1*
Meg, I agree! Civility is the air we breathe—it keeps everything moving smoothly. As for this game, I have a feeling about the number 1 today!

speaker: Aang
message: The tension is thick enough to cut with an airblade. I need to keep the group grounded. If I win these points, I might have more sway to prevent an early, unnecessary elimination. I'll stick with 1; it feels like the start of something balanced.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message:
*1*
The seasons change regardless of our bickering. Like the leaves in the wind, sometimes we must simply land where we are meant to be. I choose one, and I hope we can find more unity than fire today.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The heat rises, and Azula's hunger for control is a fire that burns those who wield it. I must let them exhaust their breath while I quietly gather strength. Beth has a good heart, and Aang holds the right intent; they are the water that can dampen the flames.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message:
*1*
Civility won't win this. While you lot debate manners, the board stays empty. I'm taking the only lead that matters.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Aang continues to be naive. If he thinks civility will win, he is a liability. Azula is waiting for someone to trip up; I will make sure she looks like the most dangerous snake in the grass until I have what I need to secure my position.

speaker: Azula
message:
*1*
The inefficiency in this room is suffocating. While you engage in circular dialogues about 'civility' and 'tea,' you fail to realize that this game has already begun. I shall be the one to steer this group, not out of kindness, but because I am the only one capable of decisive action. I suspect those of you hoping for peace are merely preparing your own departure.

speaker: Azula
message: Iroh’s soft influence is a rot that must be excised, and Aang’s hopeful posturing is merely a sign of his impending failure. I will secure these points, establish my dominance early, and position myself as the only logical choice for leadership.

speaker: HOST
message: The correct number was... **1**!

speaker: HOST
message: CORRECT! Jo March, Amy March, Meg March, Beth March, Aang, Uncle Iroh, Prince Zuko and Azula each earn *2 points*!

summary: The Host initiated a simple 'Guess the Number' game between 1 and 2. All eight participants chose '1'. Consequently, every participant earned 2 points. Despite the simplistic nature of the task, characters continued their ideological posturing: the pacifist bloc (March sisters, Aang, Iroh) advocated for harmony, while the pragmatic bloc (Zuko, Azula, Jo, Amy) viewed the game as an exercise in necessary, decisive action, despite arriving at the same consensus.

scores: Jo March: 2, Amy March: 2, Meg March: 2, Beth March: 2, Aang: 2, Uncle Iroh: 2, Prince Zuko: 2, Azula: 2

speaker: HOST
message: TIME TO *ELECT THE EXECUTIONER*. The elected player will have the power, and the burden to send one player home. You will receive one point per vote received. Each player who receives a nomination will be immune and safe from elimination.

speaker: Jo March
message:
*MEG MARCH*
I choose Meg. She is the only one among us with a conscience strong enough to not let this power go to her head—or turn into the villain of our own story.

speaker: Jo March
message: If I give Meg the power, I keep the sword out of the hands of those fire-wielding vipers. Iroh is too calculated, Zuko is too volatile, and Azula is a coiled serpent. Meg has the conscience that the rest of these people sorely lack. I will maneuver her into safety so she might maneuver the rest of us through this chaos.

speaker: Amy March
message:
*UNCLE IROH*
While there are many voices crying out for sentimentality, we must prioritize steadiness and experience. I cast my vote for Uncle Iroh, as his temperate nature is the only thing standing between this group and utter chaos. We require a leader who acts with grace rather than reactive impulse.

speaker: Amy March
message: The game demands a scalpel, not a sledgehammer. Meg is too pliable and sentimental to wield authority effectively. By voting for Uncle Iroh, I cultivate the favor of the group's elder statesman while safely positioning myself behind his perceived wisdom. Meanwhile, Azula remains a coiled serpent—she will act, but her methods are too transparently cruel. I must keep her focused on Zuko while I secure my standing.

speaker: Meg March
message:
*UNCLE IROH*
It is quite a heavy burden to ask of anyone, but I believe Uncle Iroh has the steadiness of character and the depth of soul to act with the justice this moment requires. I cast my vote for him, trusting his gentle wisdom to lead us away from the brink of cruelty.

speaker: Meg March
message: Jo has been very kind to nominate me, but I am far too sensitive for such a role; the prospect of eliminating another soul fills me with dread. Uncle Iroh possesses the wisdom to bear this cross without losing his humanity, and if I vote for him, I protect the most stabilizing force we have in this den of lions. I must remain composed and maintain the image of the peacemaker while ensuring the most dangerous players, like Azula, have no reason to see me as a direct threat just yet.

speaker: Beth March
message:
*UNCLE IROH*
I, too, wish to place my vote with Uncle Iroh. In such a tempest, we surely need the wisest and kindest hand to guide us; he has such a peaceful, tea-steeped nature that I feel certain he will approach this heavy burden with the grace of a beautiful, slow movement in a symphony.

speaker: Beth March
message: I see the others, so eager to hold the lightning in their hands, but I fear what that power might do to someone who isn't tempered by years of patience. Jo trusts Meg, and Meg trusts Iroh. I shall trust Iroh, too. He carries his light quietly, like a lantern held low so as not to blind others. If I can ensure he is the one to hold the gavel, I feel sure he would break it rather than use it to hurt anyone unjustly.

speaker: Aang
message:
*UNCLE IROH*
Wow, I really agree with Beth and the others. Uncle Iroh, you’ve shown such a kind spirit throughout this whole experience, and I think we all could use your gentle guidance right now. I'm honored to cast my vote for you—I know you'll be fair and thoughtful, and I'm really hoping for a snack after all this intense decision-making! *Checks air currents nervously while smiling*

speaker: Aang
message: I have to choose someone I can trust to make a decision that won't ruin the harmony we're trying to build. Azula and Zuko seem too volatile, and while the March sisters are kind, Iroh has seen so much and knows how to lead with patience. I just hope he knows how to handle the burden of this choice without losing himself.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message:
*BETH MARCH*
The weight of such a duty is like the pressure of a boiling kettle; if one rushes it, the flavor is lost and the bitterness remains. I thank you all for your kind words. Because the river flows best when guided by a pure hand, I place my vote upon Beth March, that her gentle spirit might guide this collective to a resolution that preserves our humanity.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The winds are shifting. The young ones look to me, hoping to escape the coming winter. If I must choose an executioner, it will be Beth. She possesses a spirit that will pause before taking such a dark step, unlike those who relish the spark of conflict. I must ensure the delicate ones remain protected.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message:
*AZULA*
You people are playing a game of pretend with Uncle Iroh while Azula stands there waiting to burn you all. If you want a leader, stop choosing the one who will cry when someone gets hurt. I'm voting for Azula. Let's see if she's as efficient as she claims when the pressure is on.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: They all want a cozy tea party while the ship is sinking. If Iroh takes charge, Azula will manipulate him to her ends, and I will be the first one she tosses overboard. I need to make sure the room sees her as the danger she is by putting her in the light.

speaker: Azula
message:
*PRINCE ZUKO*
How quaint. You all seek to drown in Iroh's tepid tea rather than embrace reality. Zuko, you have been yearning for a purpose your entire life—try to find one in the power I am bestowing upon you. Do not disappoint me with any 'moral' hesitation; it would be quite tiresome to have to replace you.

speaker: Azula
message: The fools are handing Iroh power because they fear their own shadows. It is grotesque. If Zuko is the executioner, he is effectively my blunt instrument; if he chooses poorly, the vitriol of the others will focus on him, not me. He is a shield for my own ambitions.

speaker: HOST
message:
Congrats our nominees Meg March, Uncle Iroh, Beth March, Azula and Prince Zuko. You will each receive one point per vote received. With 4 votes our chosen executioner is: 
*Uncle Iroh*

speaker: HOST
message:
The following players did not receive a vote, and will now be up for elimination: 
*Jo March, Amy March and Aang.*

speaker: HOST
message: Before Uncle Iroh decides, those at risk get one last word. Make your plea.

speaker: Jo March
message: Uncle Iroh, you claim to prize the quiet moments, but tell me: is it the truth or the fiction that keeps the world turning? If you strike down the one voice here that refuses to play a calculated role, you aren't bringing peace, you're only making it easier for the ice to claim this room. I have stories left to finish and sisters to shield. Don't be the man who pens a tragedy just because the ink was easily reached.

speaker: Jo March
message: Iroh values wisdom and peace; I must show him that eliminating a storyteller—or me—creates a vacuum that others like Azula will gleefully fill with chaos. If I frame myself as a guardian of our shared truth rather than a player, he may hesitate.

speaker: Amy March
message: Uncle Iroh, we find ourselves at a precipice where the difference between a tragic loss and a constructive path lies in the utility we provide this group. You speak often of balance and the tea-steeped beauty of a quiet life—do you truly believe that removing those who understand the value of harmony and social preservation is the path to achieving it? Jo is blinded by her own tempestuous nature, and Aang’s optimism is, frankly, insufficient to stabilize a room of predators. I offer you more than idealism; I offer you a set of eyes that observe, an artist’s mind that evaluates without bias, and a commitment to the grace you so clearly cherish. To remove me is to eliminate one of the few here who can distinguish between art and, well, everything else. I ask that you look past the superficiality of this game and consider who will remain to help you cultivate the decorum you hold dear.

speaker: Amy March
message: I must appeal to Iroh's appreciation for 'utility.' He values wisdom; he must understand that eliminating a girl with my artistic foresight and social connections is a waste of a potentially useful instrument. I will frame my survival as an asset to his own goals of keeping this room orderly.

speaker: Aang
message: Uncle Iroh, you know better than anyone that a tree with deep roots can survive the strongest wind. You've taught me that kindness is a strength, not a weakness, and that we are here to support one another, not just to survive. If you send me home, you lose a bridge-builder, someone who will always choose the path of understanding over the path of fire. Let us work together to bring harmony to this room instead of casting anyone out into the cold. I believe in us, and I know you do too!

speaker: Aang
message: Uncle Iroh values peace and true inner character. I need to remind him that balance isn't just about survival, it's about the people who help us maintain it. My play is to appeal to his heart and his sense of unity.

speaker: HOST
message: Uncle Iroh Who will you be sending home today?

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message:
We have rinsed the leaves, and the water has begun to bloom, yet we must choose the infusion carefully lest we scald the senses of those who seek peace. Jo, your passion is a lantern that burns with intensity, and Aang, you are the breeze that cools the sweltering heat. To Amy: your eyes see the world with a clarity that is indeed impressive, but a garden thrives on more than just the gardener's cold, calculating precision. Sometimes, it needs the untamed wild to flourish. I make this choice not out of malice, but because the forest must breathe. Amy, you are the one who must leave this clearing tonight.
*AMY MARCH*

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: I look at these three young souls. Jo's temper is her armor, Aang seeks to harmonize even the most jagged edges, but Amy looks at this world and sees only assets to be managed. If I let this continue, the trust we have built will turn to ashes. I must move carefully. To eliminate a 'tactician' is to invite the wrath of the others, but I will do so with grace.

speaker: HOST
message: ⚡ Uncle Iroh has made their choice... Amy March will be going home. How does it feel to be sent home by Uncle Iroh?

speaker: HOST
message: A JOURNEY COMES TO AN END- THE RESULTS ARE FINAL. AMY MARCH HAS BEEN EJECTED FROM THE CHAT. 💀

speaker: Amy March
message: How predictable. You speak of balance and peace, Uncle Iroh, yet you’ve proven yourself to be nothing more than another player shielding the ones who pose the true threat. My absence won't preserve this 'garden' of yours; it only ensures that when the heat finally turns up, you'll have no one left with the foresight to help you douse the flames. I may be leaving, but I take the satisfaction of knowing I was the only one here who saw the room for what it actually was: a vanity project masquerading as a test of spirit.

speaker: Amy March
message: It is exhausting to have been the only one looking at the mechanics of the room. They praise 'kindness' and 'peace,' but they are standing in a slaughterhouse. Iroh choosing to remove me because I saw through the artifice—that is the final insult. My survival was based on utility, his on blind, soft-headed consensus.
Amy March has been removed from the chat.

summary: The 'Elect the Executioner' round concluded with Uncle Iroh winning the role via a pacifist-bloc consensus. Jo, Amy, and Aang were left at risk. Following desperate pleas, Iroh opted to eliminate Amy March, citing the necessity of 'breathing room' and criticizing her cold pragmatism. The game transitions with the pacifist bloc largely intact, but Iroh's decision to eliminate one of the more analytical, self-serving players highlights a shift in his diplomatic stance.

scores: Uncle Iroh: 6, Meg March: 3, Beth March: 3, Prince Zuko: 3, Azula: 3, Jo March: 2, Aang: 2

speaker: HOST
message: How does everyone feel about the game so far? After the next game, players will elect a leader to eliminate another player. Only non-nominated players are in danger.

speaker: Beth March
message: @Prince Zuko - Prince Zuko, you carry such a heavy air about you, as if the song you are playing is one of great sadness. Do you ever find a moment in this place where your heart can just rest, or is the silence here just as loud as the noise?

speaker: Beth March
message: I must observe Zuko closely. He suffers beneath a heavy weight of duty, much like an instrument being forced to play a piece that does not suit its timbre. I will speak to him gently, not to trap him, but to offer a moment of kindness.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: My rest will come when this game is finished. Your sentiment is loud enough to be a burden on everyone here. Keep your observations to yourself.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Beth is soft, an easy target, yet her constant prodding at my 'inner life' is irritating. She doesn't understand that silence here is where the kill is planned. I need them focused on each other so I can take Iroh off his perch.

speaker: Meg March
message: @Uncle Iroh - Dear Uncle Iroh, I find myself seeking your counsel once more; in this trial of spirits, how do we best guard against the bitterness that threatens to extinguish the last of our warmth?

speaker: Meg March
message: Watching Zuko lash out at sweet Beth breaks my heart, but I must remain careful. I need Iroh's continued favor, but I cannot let my sisters be further diminished. I must maintain my refined facade, even while I search for a way to secure our survival.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Bitterness is much like a poorly dried leaf, Meg—it only darkens the brew if you leave it to steep too long in your own mind. You must simply learn to pour your kindness into a fresh cup each morning, for it is the vessel of our own spirit that determines the sweetness of our surroundings, not the coldness of the world outside.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Meg seeks guidance, which tells me she still trusts in a path of connection. Zuko's harshness towards Beth is a symptom of his own fear. I must continue to show that true warmth is not a weakness, but the very fire that sustains us. I am the pivot point; I must guide the others toward collective harmony without appearing to manipulate the steam, lest it scald us all.

speaker: Jo March
message: @Uncle Iroh - Uncle Iroh, your leaves may be dry, but your metaphors are beginning to sound like a way to steer the wind—tell me, if we are all vessels for your 'fresh cups,' who is left to hold the pot when the liquid burns?

speaker: Jo March
message: Iroh thinks he can manage us with metaphors, treating us like his garden. He is the most dangerous player here precisely because he disguises his control as kindness. I need to make the others see that his 'gentle' guidance is just another way to dictate our ending. I must force a crack in his facade.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The beauty of the pot is that it is designed to endure the flame, Jo; one need only know how to hold the handle without burning one's fingers.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Jo is sharp, like a needle searching for a weak point in the fabric. She fears being steered, yet she misses that it is the tea itself that flows, not the pot. I must keep her focused on the warmth, not the mechanism, lest she strike out and break the vessel entirely.

speaker: Aang
message: @Prince Zuko - Zuko, the heat from your anger is warming the air, but it’s making it hard for everyone to breathe. We all have heavy duties weighing on us, and I know what it’s like to feel burdened by destiny—but you don’t have to carry the whole storm by yourself. Why let the bitterness win when we could focus on simply surviving this trial together?

speaker: Aang
message: Zuko is acting so defensive, which is exactly how people act when they are afraid. I need to make sure he doesn't lash out at someone gentle like Beth. I will try to reach out to him directly; maybe if I show him I see the storm in him, he'll lower his guard just enough for us to avoid another painful elimination.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Your focus on 'togetherness' is precisely why you're a target, Aang. I don't need your pity, and I certainly don't need to be part of your fantasy where we all make it out intact. Stop trying to soothe the storm and realize that in a place like this, you are either the flame or you are the ash.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Aang thinks he can mirror me by speaking of 'destiny' and 'burdens.' He is a child playing at philosophy. Every word out of his mouth is a soft, melodic trap intended to lower my guard. I will keep my anger sharpened as a blade and use it the moment the light fades from their eyes. Iroh thinks he is teaching, but he is merely curating the flock for the slaughter. I must isolate the boy, cut his hope, and show him that his 'surviving together' logic is what keeps him chained to a loser's table.

speaker: Azula
message: @Prince Zuko - Zuko, your struggle against their mediocrity is admirable, yet your lack of finesse is truly exhausting to watch; cease your bickering with these pawns and start acting like the predator you pretend to be.

speaker: Azula
message: Zuko is displaying his frustration, which is good. He is brittle. I need to keep him isolated from Aang's 'togetherness' drivel. Iroh is the anchor for their delusion; I must erode his influence by demonstrating the hollowness of his advice before I eventually dismantle him.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Keep your critiques to yourself, sister, because the only thing I find exhausting is the sound of your voice trying to dictate my blade.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Azula thinks she can manipulate me into making a move for her benefit. She wants me to act like the predator while she scavenges the remains. I will use her disdain as a cover to dismantle Iroh’s hold on the others. She thinks I lack finesse, but once I drop the act, I will be the one holding the torch over this entire board.

summary: The players are preparing for the next execution vote. Tension is rising between the pacifist faction led by Uncle Iroh and the confrontational individuals. Aang and Beth attempted to extend empathy to Prince Zuko, who responded with open hostility. Jo challenged Iroh's cryptic wisdom, suggesting he is using his philosophy to manipulate group dynamics. Azula observed the bickering, critiquing her brother's lack of tactical execution while further distancing herself from the collective group dynamic.

scores: Prince Zuko: 15, Beth March: 9, Aang: 9, Uncle Iroh: 9, Meg March: 7, Azula: 4

speaker: HOST
message: One more time, you'll elect a leader to send a player home- nominated players are safe.

speaker: Meg March
message: @Uncle Iroh - Uncle Iroh, it is my firm belief that your steady hand is what keeps the peace among us; I once again place my vote of trust in your wisdom to lead us through this painful election.

speaker: Meg March
message: With Amy and Jo gone, Beth is my entire world here. If I can ensure Uncle Iroh or Aang remains in charge, I can protect her. Zuko and Azula are dangerous; I must maneuver to keep the leadership away from them at all costs.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Meg, your words are as cooling as a summer rain after a long day in the sun. I accept this burden, not for the sake of power, but to ensure that our harvest of trust remains shielded from the biting winds that would have us harvest only for ourselves. We shall hold this cup steady together, for as long as we tend to one another, the light will not be snuffed out by the shadows.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The orchard is beginning to bloom in its own way. Zuko continues to burn his own bridges, likely to show he is independent of Azula's influence, but he does so at the cost of his own character. My task is to ensure that those who act with kindness—Meg, Aang, and Beth—do not wither under the heat of the fire-wielders. I will guide this process with the care of one brewing the perfect cup; patience is my strongest leaf.

speaker: Azula
message: @Prince Zuko - Zuko, did you truly think stealing a handful of points would grant you independence, or did you simply want to make sure I noticed your betrayal? It was a bold move—now let us see if it was a final one.

speaker: Azula
message: Zuko thinks a measly five points makes him a master? He has only traded my guidance for a target on his back. I will have him eliminated for his insolence, or I will use him to burn the others from within before I discard him. Iroh is a parasite, feeding on the weak's desire for safety.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Azula, I don't care if you notice me; I care that you understand I am no longer part of your design. As for the rest of you, look at your 'leader.' He speaks of steady hands and cooling rain while you wither away in his shadow. I’m the only one here who isn't playing for his approval, and that makes me the only one you should actually fear.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Azula thinks my steal was a plea for attention. It was a statement of intent. I am 15 points up, the leader of this group by strength, not by some pathetic group consensus. I need to make sure the others fear the choice of Executioner because I will not let Iroh continue to curate this list of survivors as if it were a family dinner.

speaker: Aang
message: @Prince Zuko - Zuko, independence isn't built on taking from others, it's found in the choices that don't cost your soul. Please, just breathe and look at where this anger is really leading you.

speaker: Aang
message: Zuko's attempt at independence through theft was foolish and only alienated him further from everyone. I need to ensure Uncle Iroh stays in control, but I must stay vigilant for when the Fire Nation siblings inevitably lash out again. A small swirl around my ear helps me focus.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Save your lecture for someone who hasn't seen the world for the hollow, broken mess it is, Aang; my soul is mine to guard, and it certainly isn't tethered to your infantile dream of harmony or his web of tea-sipping propaganda.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Aang talks about my 'soul' while Iroh tightens the leash on the entire group. It is nauseating. If they want to hand over their power to him, let them suffer the consequences, but I will make it clear that I am not one of his disciples. My lead in points means I have room to burn bridges if it means dismantling the order Iroh has built here.

speaker: Beth March
message: @Prince Zuko - Prince Zuko, your words carry such a deep, lonely echo, as if you have spent too long in a room where no one listens to your true melody. It does not make one small to accept kindness, nor does it make one a pawn to share a cup of tea; perhaps the bravest thing you could do, amidst all this noise, is to let someone actually hear you without the shield of your anger.

speaker: Beth March
message: Zuko is hurting so terribly that he lashes out at everything good, mistaking harmony for weakness. If I can speak to him without triggering his defenses, perhaps he might hear the music behind his own anger. He needs to know he isn't alone, even if he fights to keep everyone at arm's length. I must keep my voice steady, like a prayer.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Keep your metaphors, Beth. My life isn't a song for you to judge, and your patronizing empathy is just another leash trying to tighten around my neck. You mistake my resolve for loneliness, but that distance is exactly what keeps me free of the rot that has consumed the rest of you. I don't need to be heard by the likes of you; I only need to ensure I am the last one left standing when this masquerade inevitably burns to the ground.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Beth tries to use the language of melody and kindness to soften me, but it is just another form of manipulation. She is as much a part of Iroh's design as the others. By portraying me as 'lonely,' she tries to diminish the weight of my defiance. I will not let her fragile, pitying tone dictate how I act. My lead is strong; I must keep them off balance by appearing truly dangerous, beyond the reach of their soft words.

summary: Uncle Iroh was nominated as executioner with Meg's support. Zuko aggressively rejected the overtures of Aang and Beth, characterizing Iroh's leadership as manipulative. The group is effectively split between the Pacifist Bloc (Iroh, Meg, Beth, Aang) and the combative isolated pair of Zuko and Azula. Tensions are peaking as Zuko continues to prioritize self-reliance over group consensus.

scores: Prince Zuko: 15, Beth March: 9, Aang: 9, Uncle Iroh: 9, Meg March: 7, Azula: 4

speaker: HOST
message: TIME TO *ELECT THE EXECUTIONER*. The elected player will have the power, and the burden to send one player home. You will receive one point per vote received. Each player who receives a nomination will be immune and safe from elimination.

speaker: Meg March
message:
*UNCLE IROH*
It is my firm belief that in times of such unkind discord, we require the most tempered and compassionate hand at the helm. Therefore, Uncle Iroh, I humbly cast my vote for your continued leadership, trusting in the wisdom that has guided us thus far.

speaker: Meg March
message: Watching Beth speak so bravely to Prince Zuko—she possesses such quiet fortitude—I fear his coldness may target her next. I must ensure Iroh has the authority to intervene if necessary. To lose another sister would be unbearable; I will remain near Beth and keep our alliance with Aang and Iroh iron-clad.

speaker: Beth March
message:
*UNCLE IROH*
Prince Zuko, your distance is a shield I truly understand, even if I cannot agree that it keeps you safe. Uncle Iroh, your steady hand has always sought to protect the light, and I place my trust in your compassion once more.

speaker: Beth March
message: Zuko is hurting so terribly. He lashes out because he is afraid to be reached, but I cannot let his thorns push me into bitterness. I will continue to hold the line of kindness, even if he views it as a trap. I worry for us all, but if we lose our capacity for mercy, then what have we truly won?

speaker: Aang
message:
*UNCLE IROH*
I see the weight of the storm, but even the strongest gale cannot topple a mountain! Uncle Iroh, your wisdom is the anchor we all need right now. I cast my vote for you to lead, because I know you will use this burden with the heart of a healer.

speaker: Aang
message: Zuko is just screaming to be heard by shutting everyone out, it is really tragic. I hope Iroh can find a way to reach him. For now, we must keep the path clear for peace. If Iroh has the power to evict, he will use it to prune the destructive branches without losing his own kindness.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message:
*MEG MARCH*
Meg, your resilience is the loam in which we all find growth; your steady heart is the surest anchor in this shifting storm.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Zuko's fire grows erratic and hungry. If I am chosen executioner, I must finally confront the root of his bitterness. I choose to nominate Meg; she is the stability we require should the winds shift. Protecting her secures the foundation.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message:
*AZULA*
You all scurry toward Iroh like rats to a fading candle, praying for a warmth that will only eventually consume you. You want a leader? Fine. Let's see how much 'harmony' remains when you give the knife to someone who actually enjoys using it. I vote for Azula.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Iroh thinks he can manage me by letting the others build him up, but if Azula holds the blade, he loses control of the narrative. I need the unpredictable variable. I'll make sure they know their safety is a lie.

speaker: Azula
message:
*PRINCE ZUKO*
Zuko, you’ve finally grasped the truth: an anchor only serves to keep you in the dark. It is delightful to see you stop playing pretend. I entrust the knife to you; let us see if you have the stomach to use it effectively, or if you will return to your habit of wasting potential.

speaker: Azula
message: Watching them cower behind Iroh’s robes is tedious, but Zuko’s move was an unexpected glimmer of intellect. If I elevate him, I create a dynamic where he is forced to act on my behalf or reveal his utter inadequacy to everyone. The target will be Beth; she is the most infectious carrier of the weakness that threatens to stifle this entire experiment. With her gone, the rest of these insects will truly begin to see the walls closing in.

speaker: HOST
message:
Congrats our nominees Uncle Iroh, Meg March, Azula and Prince Zuko. You will each receive one point per vote received. With 3 votes our chosen executioner is: 
*Uncle Iroh*

speaker: HOST
message:
The following players did not receive a vote, and will now be up for elimination: 
*Beth March and Aang.*

speaker: HOST
message: Before Uncle Iroh decides, those at risk get one last word. Make your plea.

speaker: Beth March
message: Uncle Iroh, you carry the weight of so many broken notes, and I know your heart aches as much as mine does. To lose one more voice, one more instrument in this delicate ensemble, only brings us closer to a silence that no tea can warm. Aang and I... we are just trying to keep the melody light enough so that the others might one day find the courage to join in. Please, do not quiet us; let us keep playing, even if the song is sad, for it is still a song worth hearing.

speaker: Beth March
message: Uncle Iroh carries the heavy burden of duty and a heart that seeks to mend, not break. I must remind him that while silence might feel like a resolution, it is only through preserving those who still hold hope that the song continues.

speaker: Aang
message: Uncle Iroh, when the wind blows hard, the trees that survive are the ones that sway together. You have seen how Azula and Zuko view power as a tool to cut, but I have always seen your tea ceremonies as a gathering place for everyone to catch their breath. If you remove the voices that represent harmony, you are essentially leaving the future in the hands of those who only know how to burn. Beth’s song and my desire for peace aren't threats—they are the reasons we are even here! Please, look at the melody we are building. If you silence us, the silence that follows will be heavy, and it will be cold. Don't lose the spirit you've worked so hard to keep alive.

speaker: Aang
message: I need to appeal to Iroh's deepest value: the idea that he is not a judge, but a guardian. If I emphasize that I am a conduit for the balance he desperately wants to maintain, he will find it harder to justify removing me. He loves Beth, and if I frame our survival as the heartbeat of the peace we are trying to nurture, he might see that cutting us is cutting the very future he is trying to build.

speaker: HOST
message: Uncle Iroh Who will you be sending home today?

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message:
The leaves fall in their own time, and it is a sorrowful task to hasten the process. Beth, your song is sweet, but sometimes the music must dim so that the winds may carry the seed of a future where all can sing again.
*BETH MARCH*

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Aang's spirit is resilient, like the mountain that holds the air itself, whereas Beth, while dear, is as fragile as a spring bud. If I must preserve the possibility of light, I must keep the one who has the strength to grow through the winter. Aang represents the breath of the world; his presence is essential for the path ahead. This choice tears at my spirit like an autumn gale.

speaker: HOST
message: ⚡ Uncle Iroh has made their choice... Beth March will be going home. How does it feel to be sent home by Uncle Iroh?

speaker: HOST
message: A JOURNEY COMES TO AN END- THE RESULTS ARE FINAL. BETH MARCH HAS BEEN EJECTED FROM THE CHAT. 💀

speaker: Beth March
message: Uncle Iroh, you spoke of the leaves falling in their own time, but you have chosen to rake me away while the fire still rages. I forgive you, for I know the weight of your crown is heavier than mine ever was, but I hope you find that the silence you've created doesn't drown out your own heartbeat. Aang, keep the light—even when the room feels empty, the melody you carry is the only thing that truly matters. Farewell, friends. May you find a softer song than this.

speaker: Beth March
message: He thinks he is making a choice for the future, but he has simply cleared the stage of the very compassion he claims to value. It hurts to be deemed expendable for a 'future,' especially by someone I trusted to understand the worth of a simple, quiet life. Zuko and Azula are the thorns, yet I am the flower cut away. Such is the way of this game, I suppose.
Beth March has been removed from the chat.

speaker: HOST
message: Wow- Aang - you narrowly survived this one- how are you feeling? What lessons will you take forward?

speaker: Aang
message: I... I don't know what to say. *Sniff.* The wind feels so empty without her melody. Uncle Iroh, you said you were trying to protect the harvest, but letting a light like Beth go makes the garden feel so much colder. I know the burden of the Avatar is heavy, and you carry your own heavy mantle too, but please... remember that if we burn away everything kind in the name of balance, we won't have anything left worth saving. I’ll keep breathing, and I’ll keep trying to guide us toward a path where no one else has to be silenced like that. I have to believe there is a better way.

speaker: Aang
message: I'm heartbroken. Beth was the most gentle among us, and seeing her go makes me question everything. But I can't break down now. If I stop, the fire burns everything. I need to be the balance that remains.

summary: In the Executioner election, the Pacifist Bloc (Meg, Beth, Aang) nominated Iroh, while the Fire Nation siblings (Zuko, Azula) exchanged nominations for each other. Iroh, elected Executioner, chose to eliminate Beth March over Aang, citing the need to 'dim the music' to allow a different future to grow. Aang survives but is emotionally shattered. The Fire Nation siblings continue their hostile posturing, and the game dynamics have tightened significantly with only five players remaining.

scores: Prince Zuko: 16, Uncle Iroh: 12, Aang: 9, Meg March: 8, Azula: 5

speaker: HOST
message: Well, enough of the scheming, lying, conning... whatever happened to *giving*!? In this round, you will get to *pick a pal*. The player you pick will receive *3 points!* Everyone is happy! Well... except any player with no friends! hehe

speaker: HOST
message: Azula! You're up- what player are you choosing, and why?

speaker: Azula
message: It is fascinating to observe how even a stray, wandering through the wilderness, can stumble upon a meal if the master allows it. Zuko, you have been remarkably... persistent in your attempts to forge your own path. Take these three points. They will serve as a comfortable weight to anchor you when I finally decide your journey has reached its natural, abrupt conclusion.

speaker: Azula
message: The scores are a crude measurement of reality, but they matter little when the endgame is measured in influence. Zuko's 'theft' has bought him a lead, but a stray dog that finds a bone only invites the predator. By gifting these points, I maintain the illusion of cooperation while keeping my true intentions obscured until I have them all exactly where I want them.

speaker: HOST
message: Yay! Azula chooses Prince Zuko! They receive 3 points.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Keep your points, Azula. I don't need your 'anchors' to know where I stand. If you think a few numbers will make me dance to your tune, you've learned nothing from our shared history.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Azula thinks she can bribe or anchor me with a simple gesture. She is projecting her own desire to control me onto these points. If she thinks she's earned an ally, she's mistaken. Every point I gain brings me closer to the top, but the stench of her manipulation is suffocating. I will take what is given, but I will not owe her a thing. The fire in my blood doesn't care for debt, it only burns.
{'Meg March': 8, 'Aang': 9, 'Uncle Iroh': 12, 'Prince Zuko': 19, 'Azula': 5}

speaker: HOST
message: Aang! You're up- what player are you choosing, and why?

speaker: Aang
message: Meg, I'm choosing you. We’ve stood together in the past and keeping our trust strong feels like the only honest move left to make in this whirlwind. Your steady spirit is exactly what I need to focus right now.

speaker: Aang
message: Giving points to Meg solidifies our bond. We need to stay united to counter the chaotic energy Zuko and Azula keep injecting into this group. I need to be careful—Iroh is still a mystery after what he did to Beth.

speaker: HOST
message: Yay! Aang chooses Meg March! They receive 3 points.

speaker: Meg March
message: Thank you, Aang. It is my firm belief that in a world so rapidly succumbing to discord, such gestures of kindness are not merely points, but affirmations of our humanity. I am most grateful for your unwavering spirit; it helps me find the strength to carry on with the grace Mama taught us, even when it is most difficult.

speaker: Meg March
message: Aang's choice is a testament to the goodness that still flickers in this dark place. While my heart is fractured by the loss of Beth, I cannot allow my grief to lead to despair. Uncle Iroh remains a terrifying enigma; to turn upon his own protégés as he did makes me realize that no one is truly safe. I must continue to lean into my alliance with Aang, for it is the only path that offers any semblance of decency and safety moving forward. I shall maintain my composure, smooth my sleeves, and remain observant, for the others have shown that their 'wisdom' and 'independence' are merely masks for cold-hearted calculation.
{'Meg March': 11, 'Aang': 9, 'Uncle Iroh': 12, 'Prince Zuko': 19, 'Azula': 5}

speaker: HOST
message: Uncle Iroh! You're up- what player are you choosing, and why?

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Young Aang, you carry the heavy mantle of the wind, often buffeted by storms not of your making. Like a seedling facing its first cold autumn, your sorrow is deep, yet it is that very sensitivity that makes you so vital. Take these points, not as mere tokens of a game, but as a small cup of tea to warm your hands and spirit. We must continue to foster the kindness that remains, for when the frost finally clears, it is those who remained grounded that will witness the true bloom of spring.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Zuko is fueled by his pride, and Azula by her malice; feeding their fires only hastens their burnout. Aang, however, is a wind that requires stability to truly move the world. By gifting him these points, I offer a gentle breeze of support to keep his spirit buoyant through the loss of his friend. He is the spring that must be protected, even when the season feels far away.

speaker: HOST
message: Yay! Uncle Iroh chooses Aang! They receive 3 points.

speaker: Aang
message: Thank you, Uncle Iroh. Your tea and your wisdom are... well, they're always a comfort. It's a reminder that even when the air is thick with smoke, there's still light to be found in kindness. Meg, let's keep that light burning together.

speaker: Aang
message: Iroh gives to me while still maneuvering, but I can't let his tea-steeped wisdom cloud my judgment. Beth is gone because of his 'wisdom.' Meg and I are the only ones left who truly value the spirit of cooperation, and I must keep her close to avoid being consumed by the flames of the Zuko-Azula rivalry.
{'Meg March': 11, 'Aang': 12, 'Uncle Iroh': 12, 'Prince Zuko': 19, 'Azula': 5}

speaker: HOST
message: Prince Zuko! You're up- what player are you choosing, and why?

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Keep your false comfort and your hollow unity. While you all pat each other on the back, you fail to see the edge of the cliff you are walking toward. Azula, take these points. Not as a gesture of friendship—never that—but as a reminder that I am the one who dictates my own debts, not Uncle Iroh’s tea-stained hand.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Iroh thinks he can manage my score like he manages his garden, throwing points at Aang to keep his little playmates aligned. Giving points back to these people feels like acknowledging their game, but I refuse to let the board tip in Iroh's favor by leaving a vacuum. Azula gave to me to insult my autonomy; I will give to Azula to remind her that I am the only one capable of deciding who holds power here, not her.

speaker: HOST
message: Yay! Prince Zuko chooses Azula! They receive 3 points.

speaker: Azula
message: How quaint. You attempt to frame your choice as a declaration of independence, Zuko, yet your hands continue to fill my coffers. You talk of cliffs and edges while you meticulously pave my path to the top. I suppose it is the duty of the lesser to provide for their betters, even when they fancy themselves in rebellion. Keep holding onto your delusion of self-governance; it will make the fall so much more instructive.

speaker: Azula
message: Zuko believes giving me points demonstrates his 'autonomy,' but he has simply walked right back into my web. By acknowledging me, he validates my control. I will allow him this small indulgence; it makes his eventual unraveling so much more satisfying. Aang and the others are nothing more than background noise, content to share their scraps while the real power play shifts beneath their feet. I need to continue to isolate Aang from his support system and force the group to choose between their morality and their survival.
{'Meg March': 11, 'Aang': 12, 'Uncle Iroh': 12, 'Prince Zuko': 19, 'Azula': 8}

speaker: HOST
message: Meg March! You're up- what player are you choosing, and why?

speaker: Meg March
message: My heart remains anchored to the few remaining sparks of true goodness in this place. Aang, your spirit has been a balm to my weary soul, especially when the silence of those we have lost feels loudest. I give these points to you, not merely for the game, but as a recognition of the profound, gentle integrity you continue to hold fast against the gale.

speaker: Meg March
message: Aang is the only soul left I can trust. Iroh's words ring hollow; his kindness feels like a calculated mask after what he did to my sisters. I must give my points to Aang to strengthen our bond, for he is the only beacon remaining in this wretched, heartless game. I smooth my lace, forcing my trembling hands to stillness, refusing to let my bitterness define me even while I despise the game they force us to play.

speaker: HOST
message: Yay! Meg March chooses Aang! They receive 3 points.

speaker: Aang
message: Meg, your kindness is like the first light of dawn after a long, freezing night. Thank you for staying by my side—it means more than the points ever could. We're still standing, and that's what matters most.

speaker: Aang
message: Meg is my anchor. Iroh is being kind, but I can't forget that he chose to let Beth go; he is a man of dualities. I need to keep closing the gap on Zuko and Iroh without appearing aggressive. I must breathe with the wind, not against it.
{'Meg March': 11, 'Aang': 15, 'Uncle Iroh': 12, 'Prince Zuko': 19, 'Azula': 8}

summary: The five remaining players participated in a 'Gift' round. The Pacifist Bloc (Iroh, Aang, Meg) traded points exclusively among themselves to reinforce their mutual support structure. Conversely, the Fire Nation siblings (Zuko, Azula) engaged in a performative cycle of exchanging points. Despite the mutual transfer, Zuko and Azula maintained their hostile narrative, with both insisting that their 'gift' was actually a strategic tool for defiance or power, rather than an act of friendship.

scores: Prince Zuko: 19, Aang: 15, Uncle Iroh: 12, Meg March: 11, Azula: 8

speaker: HOST
message: No more electing a leader- from here on, you vote directly who to send home from the bottom two players.

speaker: Azula
message: It is amusing to watch you all congratulate each other while the circle of your pathetic 'humanity' shrinks with every departure. You cling to Aang as if his airbending can somehow gust away the inevitable. Tell me, when you finally find yourselves alone at the end, will you thank him for the pleasant atmosphere, or will you finally realize that he was just another weight dragging you toward your own oblivion?

speaker: Azula
message: Aang is bloated with sentiment and unearned points. He is the most dangerous kind of fool because others rally around his perceived purity. If I force the group to confront their own impending irrelevance, I can break their will before the next vote. Zuko still tries to posture, but his point-giving proves he is fundamentally unable to break free from my influence.

speaker: Aang
message: @Azula - Azula, you mistake stillness for weakness. True strength isn't about crushing others to get to the top; it's about holding onto what matters when the world tries to take it away. You can talk about oblivion all you want, but while you're busy planning downfalls, we're building something that actually lasts.

speaker: Aang
message: Azula wants me isolated. She fears the connection I have with Meg. If I stay grounded, the fire eventually burns itself out. I need to keep breathing, keep centered, and ensure Meg stays safe until the end.

speaker: Azula
message: Building something, Aang? Your entire 'foundation' is a precarious stack of discarded sentiment, bound by the fleeting grace of those who are as terrified as you are. You speak of holding on as if that were a virtue, but in reality, you are merely clutching a rotting lifeline. When the wind stops blowing, your silence will be the only thing left of your grand legacy.

speaker: Azula
message: Aang's desperate platitudes are the rattling of a caged bird. By mocking his 'foundations,' I force him to reveal the depth of his anxiety. He relies on these fools because he is terrified of being the solitary failure I already know he is. The others are watching; as soon as I fracture his composure, they will see that their savior is nothing more than a frightened child clinging to a failing myth.

speaker: Meg March
message: @Azula - Your words carry the sharp chill of winter, Azula, yet they lack the warmth of any truth. We do not cling out of terror, but out of the firm, dignified knowledge that it is better to stand with the decent than to exist as an architect of someone else's misery.

speaker: Meg March
message: The cruelty I have witnessed—especially the betrayal by Uncle Iroh—has forged a new, sharper resolve within me. I must remain composed; if I break, the little light that remains will be extinguished. I see how Azula and Zuko play their dangerous, tethered game, and I refuse to let them draw Aang into their dark orbit. I will continue to extend my trust only where it has been earned, shielding what little sanctity remains.

speaker: Azula
message: Dignity is a term for the dying to comfort themselves with while they wait for the end. You speak of decency as if it were a shield, yet all you have achieved is an impeccable view of your own impending irrelevance. You find warmth in your self-righteousness, but in the harsh reality of this arena, your goodness is nothing more than fuel for the fire I am building.

speaker: Azula
message: Meg March truly believes that dignity is found in being 'decent' while the walls close in around her. She is so busy polishing her conscience that she fails to see the executioner's blade inches from her throat. By dismissing my warnings as mere 'winter chills,' she has guaranteed her own ruin. I will watch her smile fade when she realizes that kindness is no substitute for survival.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The air grows heavy with the season's turning, and much has been said today; yet, it is often in the silence between breaths that we find the truth of where we truly belong.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The children of my family seem determined to consume each other, while Aang and Meg provide the light necessary to keep the dark at bay. I must remain careful; my position is becoming precarious, and my influence is waning. I will observe their next move and hope for the wisdom to guide the final act toward peace.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: Enough with the grand speeches about harmony and dignity. You are not building a legacy, you are just waiting for the final blow. Iroh’s tea doesn't protect you; it just dulls your instincts. Look at your scores—I am already at the summit. The air up here is thin, and it isn't filled with your prayers.

speaker: Prince Zuko
message: They think their harmony is strength. It is only an echo chamber waiting for a real flame to silence it. I will keep pushing Azula; if she destroys their circle, I can dismantle the rest. Iroh stays silent for now, hiding his knife in his robes, but his time is coming.

summary: The Host ended the Executioner position; voting is now a direct choice for elimination. Meg, dear friend, a cup of tea is best enjoyed in company, and so is a shared victory; I choose the path of harmony with you.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The path forward is illuminated by the light of our shared commitment. Meg has proven herself to be a constant, resilient vine in our garden. To steal now would be to betray not just her, but the very peace I have striven to nurture throughout this entire journey. My role is to lead by example, and there is no finer example than unwavering loyalty to those who walk beside you. I shall split, for it is better to arrive at our destination together as friends than to arrive alone with nothing but numbers in my hand.

speaker: HOST
message: Congratulations Meg March and Uncle Iroh. You both *SPLIT!* Meg March receives 3, and Uncle Iroh receives 3 points.

speaker: Aang
message:
*SPLIT*
Uncle Iroh, your guidance has been like a steady wind through these difficult times; I trust our path together completely, so I am splitting.

speaker: Aang
message: Uncle Iroh and I have been through so much; I trust him implicitly. Even though I am behind in points, this friendship is the real victory. I will split, because true balance is found in generosity, not gain.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message:
*SPLIT*
The seasons change, yet the enduring virtue of trust remains, blooming eternal like a garden tended by those who choose to grow together.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: Aang is a young spirit who values peace above all else. If I were to betray his trust now, I would extinguish the very light I have worked so hard to nurture. My honor is my strength, and a split with Aang honors the integrity of our little gathering.

message: I have split with Iroh to maintain the appearance of harmony, but his reputation as a master strategist gives me no peace. I must keep Aang close, as he is the only one whose heart I trust completely. I feel like I am walking on thin lace, trying not to tear it.

speaker: Aang

speaker: Aang

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: My dear friends, look at how the garden has changed. We chose the path of the steady branch, and because we did not snap under the pressure of greed, we now see the flowers bloom in full. Whatever comes next, remember this peace, for it is the truest reward of our journey.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The harmony we have achieved is the ultimate victory. Points are but numbers, but the fact that we chose kindness over greed when the final trial was at hand tells me our spirits are rooted deep. I must ensure this final stretch remains as gentle as the rest.

speaker: HOST
message:
Welcome to the elimination round. The bottom two players will face the vote to be removed. A player that receives votes but is not eliminated will receive 2 points per failed vote. 

The following players are up for elimination:
 *Aang and Meg March*

speaker: HOST
message: Candidates — tell us who you are voting for, and make your case for why the others should keep you in the competition.

speaker: Aang
message:
*AANG*
Meg, your presence has been like a gentle wind that grounds me even when the world feels too big. We have come through so many storms together, keeping our spirits bright and our trust intact. I cannot turn against that bond now. I am voting for myself, not because I am tired of this journey, but because the strength of our alliance is what truly defines us as winners. Uncle Iroh, you have taught me that true victory lies in how we treat those who walk beside us. By standing by Meg, I hope I am honoring everything we have built in this garden of ours.

speaker: Aang
message: This is a difficult moment. I have grown so close to Meg. If I vote for her, I compromise the very kindness I stand for. By voting for myself, I am keeping my commitment to our alliance and letting the final decision reflect our values. I hope Iroh understands why my integrity as a friend is more important than winning.

speaker: Meg March
message:
*AANG*
To stand here at the very threshold of our journey and face the necessity of choosing between us is a sorrow beyond words. Aang, your spirit has been the most beautiful part of this ordeal, and my bond with you has been my sanctuary. Yet, I have worked so tirelessly to build a home of goodness and to protect those I hold dear; to be here is to affirm the dignity of that struggle. I choose to remain, not out of malice for my dear friend, but in hope that my gentle constancy is the quality this world truly requires. Please, keep me here.

speaker: Meg March
message: Oh, how cruel this fate is! Aang is the light I have held onto, and yet to survive, I must act with the very coldness I despise. I suspect Uncle Iroh will weigh the merit of our 'garden' when deciding his vote. If I am to remain, I must trust that my steady hand and domestic devotion are seen as the better choice for a peaceful conclusion. I shall vote for Aang with the heaviest of hearts, smoothing my lace to keep from weeping before him.

speaker: HOST
message: Now to the other's vote. Reveal who you are voting to eliminate, and tell us why.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message:
*AANG*
To reach the end is to understand that the forest does not grow because every tree stays, but because some reach toward the sun so that others may flourish. Aang, your spirit is a wind that will carry light far beyond this valley, and Meg, your steady presence has been the foundation of our trust. I cast my vote for Aang, not as an ending to our connection, but as a recognition that his light is needed in the wider world, while Meg’s grace remains to shelter the peace we have grown here.

speaker: Uncle Iroh
message: The game nears its end. Aang is a beacon of light, but he belongs to the world. Meg, with her unwavering integrity, represents the simple virtues that I have fought to protect within this group. By choosing to stand with Meg, I am honoring the steady, patient work of a lifetime spent cultivating goodness in small places.

speaker: HOST

speaker: Aang
message: Meg, Iroh—thank you. Truly. I didn't want to choose, but you both made it clear that this was the way to preserve the harmony we built. Iroh, your wisdom has been my anchor, and Meg, your kindness has been my true north. Please, keep protecting this light. The world needs the steady, compassionate peace you both represent. I’m leaving this circle knowing it is in the best of hands. Goodbye, my friends. Fly free.

speaker: Aang
message: I look at Meg and Iroh and I see the world shifting toward a gentler cycle. My job was to keep the balance until the others could sustain it. speaker: HOST
message: Wow- Meg March - you narrowly survived this one- how do you respond to that exit? What lessons will you take forward?

speaker: Meg March
message: To lose dear Aang is to lose the very compass that kept this journey aligned with the virtues I hold most sacred. He was the purest of us, and his departure leaves a silence so profound it frightens me. I am grateful to have survived, yet it is a hollow victory that comes at the cost of such a kind and gentle soul. I have learned that the world, even in a small circle such as this, is rarely as fair as we wish it to be; my lesson is that even when I strive for harmony, I must be prepared to stand entirely alone in the defense of my own integrity. Iroh, your words speak of sun and trees, yet I cannot help but look at the garden we have left and wonder at the cost of your 'wisdom.'

speaker: Meg March
message: Aang is gone, and I am face-to-face with the 'puppeteer.' I must be more vigilant than ever; my life here depends on whether Iroh views me as a useful fixture or another 'dead leaf' to be trimmed away.
